coredata.dc:title: Use of carbon materials for constructing a closed water treatment system
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Minh Hoang
Ngoc, Nguyen Thi Bich
Huy, Hoang Van

Dao, Van-Duong
coredata.dc:description: Water purification by solar distillation has been deemed to be a green and promising approach for the production of potable water from undrinkable water resources. Carbonaceous materials have been well-known for the application of the sorption layer in the solar-to-steam system. Herein, both carbon spheres (CSs) and carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 12h and they were used for constructing a closed water treatment system, in which CSs served as solar-thermal membranes in harvesting water and purifying Fe3+ ions in condensed water, while CDs played a role in the determination of Fe3+ ions in water. The evaporation rate of the CS membrane reaches 1.046 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 sun illumination and the efficiency treatment of Fe3+ is about 80%. The limit of detection obtained in the evaluation of Fe3+ ions in water by CDs was 3.61 μM. This work represents a significant step forward, as it opens the concept of constructing a closed water treatment system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon spheres
Solar to steam
Water treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a). SEM image of CSs with the insert image of size distribution histogram. (b) HR-TEM image of CDs with the insert image of size distribution histogram. (c) FT-IR spectra of CSs and CDs. (d) XRD patterns of CDs and CSs.

(a) Schematic diagram of the experimental setup for solar steam generation. (b) UV-VIS spectra of CS membrane. Inset image: A photo digital image of CS membrane (c) Time-dependent temperature of dry and wet CS membrane under 1 sun illumination. (d) IR image of dry and wet CS membrane under 1 sun illumination. Mass change of water obtained by CS membrane, CD membrane, and cellulose membrane under (e) 1 sun illumination and (f) in the dark.

(a) Digital images capture steam generated by CS membrane under the sunlight. (b) Comparison of water before and after purification by CS membrane. (c) Water samples before and after purification were mixed with CDs under UV light excitation. (d) PL spectrum of CDs in pure water and water before and after solar thermal purification.
Illustration of the synthesis method of CDs and CSs and their applications.

head.ce:title: Use of carbon materials for constructing a closed water treatment system

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Technology, Hanoi University of Science and Technology, Hanoi, 100000, Viet Nam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water purification by solar distillation has been deemed to be a green and promising approach for the production of potable water from undrinkable water resources. Carbonaceous materials have been well-known for the application of the sorption layer in the solar-to-steam system. Herein, both carbon spheres (CSs) and carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 12h and they were used for constructing a closed water treatment system, in which CSs served as solar-thermal membranes in harvesting water and purifying Fe3+ ions in condensed water, while CDs played a role in the determination of Fe3+ ions in water. The evaporation rate of the CS membrane reaches 1.046 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 sun illumination and the efficiency treatment of Fe3+ is about 80%. The limit of detection obtained in the evaluation of Fe3+ ions in water by CDs was 3.61 μM. This work represents a significant step forward, as it opens the concept of constructing a closed water treatment system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution by heavy metals has become an increasingly global environmental concern, particularly in countries where mineral extraction, industrial, and agricultural practices are developing [1]. These activities trigger the contamination of heavy metal ions such as Fe3+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ into water sources. Consuming metal-polluted water can result in acute or chronic diseases such as kidney failure, heart disease, and cancer [2]. Therefore, detecting and removing heavy metal ions in polluted water is imperative for water harvesting [3].

Over the past decades, carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphene oxide have been widely used in water treatment because of their high absorption capacity, low production cost, abundant sources, and large-scale production [4,5]. Recently, carbon spheres (CSs) have emerged as a new generation for water treatment and harvesting fresh-water [6–11]. Because of their high porosity, large surface area, and abundant functional groups [12–14], CSs show good adsorption for various organic (rhodamine B dye, methyl orange, levofloxacin) [15–18] and inorganic compounds (Fe, Cr, Pb) [13,19,20]. In addition, CSs offer excellent sunlight absorbance, which allows them to effectively utilize solar energy. Therefore, CSs have a great potential for harvesting freshwater using solar energy [21]. Some reports have integrated two applications of CSs to simultaneously remove contaminants and harvest fresh water. For example, Yang et al. [22] successfully synthesized a hollow carbon sphere membrane exhibiting an excellent water evaporation efficiency of 92.7% at 1 kWm−2. In addition, CSs have long-term stability and high efficiency in producing drinkable water and purifying various types of wastewater, including seawater, strong acid/alkaline water, and water-containing dyes.

Carbon-based materials have also been widely applied for the detection of heavy metal ions in water. Especially, carbon dots (CDs) have opened a new era in the sensor field owing to their fast, precise, and selective detection capacity [23]. Thanks to the quenching/enhancement effect [24,25], various heavy metals have been detected by CDs, with detection limits going down to a few nM in aqueous solutions [26], food [27], or living cells [28].

Herein, CSs and CDs were synthesized concurrently via a one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose and lemon juice (as the citric acid source) as precursors and their application in generating clean water. To the best knowledge, CSs are applied as solar absorber materials for the first time in a solar-driven water evaporation system while CDs were selected for the detection of Fe3+ ions in condensed water from water evaporation. It should be noted that in general to determine the Fe3+ ion concentration, atomic absorption spectroscopy is conducted. In this work, atomic absorption spectroscopy was released and replaced by the fluorescence detection method which just used CDs. This work represents a significant step forward, as it opens the concept of constructing a closed water treatment system.

Scheme 1 depicts the synthesis method and applications of CDs and CSs. CDs and CSs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal reaction. Firstly, 10 g glucose was added to 30 ml of lemon juice before stirring moderately for 15 min until the glucose was dissolved. This solution was then poured into a 50 ml Teflon which will be put into an autoclave to be ready for the hydrothermal process. The process was carried out under temperatures of 180 °C for 12h. After gradually cooling, the product was centrifuged at 3000 rpm to separate the black precipitate from the supernatant. The black precipitate was CSs. The CSs were then washed three times with distilled water before drying at 80 °C for 24h. The supernatant was then filtered by a Whatman paper 3 μm to collect a brown solution that contains CDs. The collected CDs and CSs were stored at room temperature for further use.

The morphology of the materials was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). CSs exhibited a 3D spherical structure with a smooth surface (Fig. 1 a). The particle size of CSs is distributed broadly in the range of 4–9 μm with an average of 6.64 (±0.04) μm. In contrast to CSs, CDs are 0D nanomaterials with a size distribution of 2–6 nm (Fig. 1b). The average size of the CDs was calculated to be 3.78 (±0.61) nm which is approximately 1800 times smaller than that of CSs.

The average sizes of both CDs and CSs in our experiment are larger than those of CDs and CSs in the previous reports (Tables S1 and S2). At the mild reaction temperature of 180 °C, CDs consist of nuclei covered by a polymer layer on their surface [29,30]. With an increase in the reaction time, the polymer layer expands larger, leading to an increase in the CD size. In this experiment, the reaction time was much longer than in the other reports so CD particles of larger size were obtained. For the synthesis of CS particles, the reaction time of 180 °C is an optimal condition for the formation of CSs [31,32], and CSs usually grow at a fast rate for a reaction time of 12h [33,34]. In addition, the higher glucose concentration in our experiment (1.85 μM) resulted in a quicker build-up of molecules toward the CSs surface [29]. Consequently, the obtained CSs particles are larger than those in the other reports. These results indicate that the hydrothermal method is feasible for the simultaneous synthesis of CDs and CSs.

Although the two materials exhibit a massive difference in spatial dimension and size, they share strikingly similar characteristics in functional groups. The strong and broad peak at around 3300 cm−1 and its shoulder peak at 2900 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra are assigned to –OH and –CH2 vibrations, respectively (Fig. 1c) [35,36]. Four oxygen-containing groups were characterized in the wavenumber region from 1000 to 1700 cm−1, including C–OH, C–O–C, C–O, and CO [30]. In addition, a sharp peak at 1650 cm−1 corresponds to CC bonding [30]. The presence of the OH group on the CDs surface originates from the good solubility of the CDs. Meanwhile, the hydrophilic OH group on the surface of CSs leverages capillary forces and promotes fluid flow on the surface of CSs [31].

The XRD pattern of CSs shows a peak at 15° indicating that the material contained highly amorphous and disordered carbon (Fig. 1d) [32]. Meanwhile, a broad hump at 23° is observed in the XRD pattern of the CDs, which is widely reported in amorphous CDs [33,34].

Fig. 2 a depicted the schematic diagram of the experimental setup for solar to steam generation. The solar steam testing system consisted of an electrical balance with a resolution of five decimal places and a Xenon lamp to simulate sunlight. A Petri disk containing 10 ml of water was put onto the electrical balance. On the water surface, there is a solar steam generation device consisting of styrofoam, a CS membrane (a diameter of 4 cm and an average thickness of 54.4 μm), and cellulose paper to create water flow to the CS membrane. During the evaporation process, the electrical balance measures the mass change.

As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the CS membrane shows excellent sunlight absorption in both dry and wet states. The absorbance values of the dry and wet CS membranes were greater than 90 and 95%, respectively. This explains the black color of the CS membrane in the inset. Under 1 sun radiation, the temperatures of the dry and wet CS membranes increased rapidly (within 2 min) to 55 and 37 °C, respectively. Both membranes remained stable at equilibrium (Fig. 2c). The results are consistent with the thermal images recorded by an IR camera as shown in Fig. 2d. The temperature at the illuminated region on the CS membrane is relatively homogeneous, with maximum temperatures of 56.2 °C and 37.8 °C for dry and wet membranes, respectively.

Steam generation of the CS membrane was carried out under 1 sun and in the dark. To verify the efficiency of CS coating in improving steam generation, we compared the efficiency of the CS membrane with that of a membrane coated with CDs and a bare cellulose membrane (Fig. S1). The preparation method for the CD membrane is described in Supporting Information. The evaporation rate of the CS membrane reaches 1.046 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 sun, which is nearly three times higher than that of the CD membrane (Fig. 2e). In addition, if we replace the CS layer on the CS membrane, the evaporation rate under 1 sun is significantly reduced to 0.124 kg m−2 h−1. Similarly, the evaporation rate of the CS membrane still exceeded that of the CD and cellulose membrane in the dark. As shown in Fig. 2f, the evaporation rate of the CS membrane achieves 0.126 kg m−2 h−1 compared to only 0.084 kg m−2 h−1 for the CD membrane and 0.045 kg m−2 h−1 for the cellulose membrane.

The efficiency of the water evaporation process was 75% and 25.6% for the CS and CD membrane, respectively. Note that the efficiency was only 7% for a device with the cellulose membrane. The obtained results indicate that coating CSs dramatically improves the efficiency of the water evaporation process. The improvement could be explained by (i) the excellent light absorbance of the CS membrane in the range from UV to IR wavelength compared to the CDs and cellulose membrane (Fig. S2), and (ii) the abundance of hydrophilic functional groups and the smooth surface of CSs facilitate the rapid water transportation from the bulk to the evaporative surface [35].

To evaluate the applicability of the CS membrane in water purification, it was exploited to remove Fe3+ ions in water under sunlight. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, water steam quickly forms and covers the outer layer of the solar steam generation device after 30 min. After one day, the precipitated water was collected. The water sample substantially changed from yellow to colorless after the purification process (Fig. 3b), indicating a significant decrease in Fe3+ ion concentration. Note that the Fe3+ concentration before the purification process was 100 μM. These results show the feasibility of generating clean water by the CS membrane.

To measure the concentration of Fe3+ ions in water, we took advantage of the excellent optical properties of the CDs and the quenching effect of the CDs toward Fe3+ ions. Fig. S3 demonstrates the absorption spectra of the CDs in the UV region with two emission peaks at 290 and 350 nm. These peaks were ascribed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic ring and the n→π* transition of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the CDs. Under excitation wavelengths from 340 to 370 nm, CDs emit a broad PL spectrum from 400 to 600 nm, with the maximum peak at 450 nm (Fig. S4). The quantum yield was calculated to be 21% (Fig. S5), which was higher than those of previous works [36–41]. These results indicate the excellent optical properties of CDs. Moreover, the CDs demonstrate great potential in detecting Fe3+ ions in water via the quenching effect. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs gradually decreased with the increasing Fe3+ ions concentration from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. S6). The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 3.61 μM based on Fig. S6, which is much lower than the permitted iron concentration in drinking water by WHO.

Fig. 3c shows the fluorescence light of the CDs mixed with the two water samples under UV light excitation. The polluted water sample emitted weak blue emission light due to the quenching effect of CDs toward a high concentration of Fe3+ ions. Meanwhile, the purified sample emits strong blue emission, showing a low concentration of Fe3+ ions after purification. The change in fluorescence signal was measured using photoluminescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3d, the emission peak of the purified sample was much higher than that of the polluted sample, indicating a low concentration of Fe3+ ions in the purified sample compared to the polluted sample. This is consistent with the observation shown in Fig. 3b.

According to the linear fitting curve in Fig. S7:
y = 0.019x - 0.218
where y is equal to F0/F −1 (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively) and x is the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The concentration of Fe3+ ions after purification was calculated to be 21.04 μM which is close to the value of 20 μM determined by the atomic absorption spectroscopy on Agilent 240FS AA spectrometer. The result indicates that the CS membrane has a huge potential for removing Fe3+ ions in polluted water, and the CDs can be applied in the quantitative determination of Fe3+ ions in polluted water.

In summary, CDs and CSs were synthesized simultaneously from natural and environment-friendly materials by one-pot hydrothermal methods at 180 °C for 12h. Both CDs and CSs showed amorphous structures with abundant hydrophilic functional groups on their surface. The average sizes of CDs and CSs were 3.78 (±0.61) nm and 6.64 (±0.04) μm, respectively. By exploiting both materials, a closed-water treatment system that detects and removes Fe3+ ions, and harvests freshwater was developed. The CDs played an important role in evaluating the concentration of Fe3+ in water, with a LOD of 3.61 μM. The evaporation rate under 1 sun of the solar-to-steam generation system using CS membrane was 1.046 kg m−2 h−1, and Fe3+ concentration decreased by approximately 80% after the evaporation process. The findings of this study illustrate a green, economical, and time-saving production technology of CDs and CSs which are further applied to a promising solar-to-steam system.

Minh Hoang Nguyen: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Nguyen Thi Bich Ngoc: Data curation, Formal analysis. Hoang Van Huy: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Quang Duy Dao: Formal analysis. Tuan Tu Le: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hai-Linh Thi Dang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Nam-Anh Tran: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Vu Quang Minh: Data curation, Formal analysis. Tran Van Tan: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Do Thi Lien: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Nguyen Thi Khanh Huyen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Van-Duong Dao: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the Asia Research Center (grant number CA.21.3A).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metal contamination in water and its possible sources
